output.final_deliverable:
I could not extract the requested article facts because there are no visited pages or merged search results provided. Missing evidence (required to complete the mandate):

1) At least two visited news-website pages (HTML/text) containing articles about "artificial intelligence regulation" from the past year. For each visited page I need the page content to extract the following explicit facts: headline, publication date, main topic/key point, and source website name.

2) URLs (if you prefer specific articles) or permission for me to perform web searches and visits so I can retrieve those pages.

What I will do once I have that evidence / permission:

- Search for recent (past 12 months) news articles about "artificial intelligence regulation." I will visit at least two different news websites (not Wikipedia). Suggested reputable sources to check: Reuters, BBC, The New York Times, The Guardian, Financial Times, Politico, Axios.

- For each article visited I will extract and list explicitly the following labeled facts:
  1. Headline: "..."
  2. Publication date: YYYY-MM-DD (exact value)
  3. Main topic / key point: short sentence summarizing the article's main claim
  4. Source website name: e.g., "Reuters"
  5. URL: full URL (if found)

- I will present the results as a numbered list of articles (one entry per article) with the four required fields for each, and then provide a concise comparative summary of the perspectives (e.g., differences in emphasis: regulation urgency, industry impact, proposed rules, government vs. industry voices).

Please tell me which of the following you prefer:
A) Allow me to search the web now and visit at least two news websites so I can extract the facts and return them; or
B) Provide 2–4 article URLs you want me to analyze (I will extract facts from those). 

If you pick A, I will proceed to search and visit and then return the extracted facts with full URLs and a comparative summary.

Missing evidence summary (short): No visited pages or search results were provided, so I cannot extract headlines, dates, topics, or source names. Provide permission to search or supply URLs.
output.action_summary: No articles were visited or provided in the merged results, so I cannot yet extract the headline, publication date, main topic, or source name for any news article about "artificial intelligence regulation" from the past year. To complete the task I need either permission to perform web searches and visit news sites now, or you can paste 2+ article URLs for me to analyze. Once I have pages to read, I will return a numbered list of articles with explicit labels for headline, exact publication date, main topic/key point, source website name, and URL, followed by a comparative summary of perspectives.

b7ed5b62-1be7-4be3-9c2a-8f7c7dcbaf7e.title: Search recent news articles about 'artificial intelligence regulation' (past 12 months)

details.query: artificial intelligence regulation news past 12 months OR "AI regulation" (site:reuters.com OR site:nytimes.com OR site:washingtonpost.com OR site:ft.com OR site:theguardian.com OR site:politico.com OR site:bloomberg.com OR site:axios.com OR site:cnn.com OR site:bbc.com)
details.intent: Find at least 4 candidate news articles (from at least 2 different reputable news websites, excluding Wikipedia) about AI regulation published within the past 12 months. Prefer pieces that discuss policy proposals, national/regional regulation (e.g., EU/US/UK), industry reaction, or legislative progress.
verification_steps: Confirm the article's publication date is within the past 12 months (compare page date and meta tags).
Confirm the domain is a recognized news outlet (not Wikipedia or a blog).
Capture the canonical URL returned by the site (do not construct or modify URLs).
Collect at least two different domains among returned URLs (to satisfy 'at least 2 different news websites').
details.why_this_node: A focused search is required to produce exact URLs for authoritative news articles. Visit nodes will consume those URLs. This step reduces uncertainty by gathering candidate sources and enabling verification before full-content extraction.
details.notes_on_data_flow: This node will produce the list of candidate URLs that subsequent visit nodes will consume. Vector DB will be consulted automatically to surface previously seen links.

results.title: Hochul Reaches Deal on A.I. Regulation in New York - The New York Times
results.description: “New York is once again leading the nation in setting a strong and sensible standard for frontier AI safety, holding the biggest developers accountable for their safety and transparency protocols,” Ms. Hochul said in a statement, adding that the reforms represent progress “as the federal government lags behind, failing to implement common-sense regulations that protect the public.” · The bill, the Responsible Artificial Intelligence Safety and Education, or Raise, Act, is the first A.I.-related state law to be enacted since President Trump signed an executive order last week aimed at reining in states’ ability to limit the artificial intelligence industry.

results.title: Backed by Anthropic, a Super PAC Group Begins an Ad Blitz in Support of A.I. Regulation - The New York Times
results.description: States and the federal government are at odds over how to regulate the technology. While President Trump has said A.I. regulation could slow down American companies and boost Chinese rivals, states are pushing ahead with their own laws.

results.title: China issues draft rules to regulate AI with human-like interaction | Reuters
results.description: BEIJING, Dec 27 (Reuters) - China's cyber regulator on Saturday issued draft rules for public comment that would tighten oversight of artificial intelligence services designed to simulate human personalities and engage users in emotional interaction.

results.title: Don’t be fooled. The US is regulating AI – just not the way you think | Sacha Alanoca and Maroussia Lévesque | The Guardian
results.description: As of late, AI leaders such as the US have doubled down on this messaging. JD Vance champions AI policy with a “deregulatory flavor”. Congress considered a 10-year ban on state AI legislation.

results.title: Trump Moves to Stop States From Regulating AI With a New Executive Order - The New York Times
results.description: California has passed a law that require the biggest A.I. models, including OpenAI’s ChatGPT and Google’s Gemini, to test for safety and to disclose the results. South Dakota passed a law banning deep fakes, which are realistic A.I.-generated videos, in political advertisements within months of an election.

results.title: South Korea’s ‘world-first’ AI laws face pushback amid bid to become leading tech power | South Korea | The Guardian
results.description: South Korea has launched what it calls ‘world-first’ laws aimed at regulating artificial intelligence.

results.title: Trump signs executive order aimed at preventing states from regulating AI | US news | The Guardian
results.description: The “Ensuring a national policy framework for artificial intelligence” order is a victory for Silicon Valley and AI companies that have lobbied against regulation of their technology, arguing that a hodgepodge of state laws would burden ...

results.title: Trump signs executive order blocking states from enforcing their own regulations around AI | CNN Business
results.description: Congress killed an earlier attempt by Republicans to prevent states from regulating AI in July. The US Senate voted nearly unanimously to remove a 10-year moratorium on the enforcement of state artificial intelligence regulations from Trump’s ...

results.title: Trump threatens funding for states over AI regulations | Reuters
results.description: New York state last month became the first to enact a law requiring online retailers that employ "surveillance pricing" to disclose their use of algorithms and customers' personal data.

results.title: From A.I. to Immigration, These New State Laws Will Take Effect in 2026 - The New York Times
results.description: One new California law, inspired by a teenager in the state who died by suicide after confiding in ChatGPT, will put in place safeguards for A.I. chatbots. The law will regulate the exposure of minors to sexual content and establish protocols ...

1ecc2c18-f44f-41c7-90cc-7f040205a0d9.title: Visit selected article #1 (top relevant URL from search results)

details.optional_url: <<use top-1 URL from previous search results (search_result_urls[0])>>
details.intent: Open the first selected news article and extract structured facts: headline, publication date, byline (if present), the article's main topic/key point (one-sentence), and source website name/domain.
target_facts: publication_date (ISO or explicit text on page)
extraction_plan: Read article page content and meta tags (og:title, meta[name='pubdate'] or article date).
Extract the first paragraph and any explicit summary lines that state the article's main claim or policy focus.
Record the domain and visible site name (e.g., Reuters, NYTimes).
Capture any explicit mentions of regulatory proposals, named legislation (e.g., 'EU AI Act'), or quotes from policymakers/industry.
verification_steps: Check that the article date falls within the past 12 months.
Cross-check headline against search snippet to ensure correct page.
If multiple dates present (updated vs published), capture both and label accordingly.
measurable_output_fields: raw_excerpt (1-2 sentences supporting the main topic)
details.why_this_node: Full-page visit is necessary to extract authoritative headline/date and to understand the article's substantive angle. This reduces uncertainty compared to relying on search snippets alone.

b0fc5fb9-6fcd-428b-8cbd-0edeea3acd38.title: Visit selected article #2 (second relevant URL from search results, different domain from article #1)

details.optional_url: <<use top-2 URL from previous search results (search_result_urls[1]) - ensure a different domain than article #1>>
details.intent: Open a second distinct news article from a different outlet and extract the same structured facts as article #1 for cross-source comparison.
extraction_plan: Extract headline and publication date from page and meta tags.
Summarize the main regulatory angle (e.g., calls for stricter rules, industry concerns, legal challenges).
Capture key quotes or policy references that illustrate the outlet's perspective.
verification_steps: Ensure the domain differs from article #1 to capture perspective variation.
Confirm the date is within the past 12 months.
Compare the page's headline and snippet to the search result for consistency.
details.why_this_node: A second site visit provides another independent data point and allows identification of differing emphases or framing in coverage. This reduces bias introduced by any single outlet.

348d1feb-5732-4638-b301-134192cef536.title: Synthesize findings and save extracted facts

details.intent: After extracting facts from the visited articles, produce a concise comparison of perspectives and save the structured article facts to memory for later retrieval.
synthesis_plan: Compare the two articles' main topics and identify points of agreement (e.g., regulatory urgency, focus on AI safety, economic concerns) and points of divergence (e.g., support for EU-style rules vs. calls for industry-led standards).
Assess tone (neutral/reporting, critical, advocacy) and note any named legislation or actors emphasized.
Produce a short summary (3-6 sentences) comparing perspectives and a 3-bullet list highlighting similarities, differences, and the most-cited policy proposals.
measurable_output_fields: confidence_level (low/medium/high) based on number of corroborating sources
details.post_think_save_plan: Call save action to store the structured article facts (headline, date, main_topic, source, url) into memory/namespace 'idea_dag:008e31e492'.
details.why_this_node: A reflective synthesis is required to meet the user's request for a comparative summary. It ensures the extracted facts are combined coherently and prepares the data for storage. This step relies on data gathered by the prior visit nodes.
